title: Methi Thepla

overview.description: A seasoned Gujarati flatbread made with wholemeal flour, gram flour and dried fenugreek leaves, cooked on a hot tawa.

method: Add the atta, gram flour, dried fenugreek leaves, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder and salt to a large bowl.
Gradually add the water, mixing until a soft dough forms.
Knead for 5–8 minutes until smooth and pliable.
Divide the dough into 8 equal portions and roll each into a smooth ball.
Roll one ball into a thin circle approximately 15–18cm in diameter.
Heat a tawa or heavy frying pan over medium-high heat.
Place the thepla in the dry pan.
Cook until small bubbles appear and the underside develops pale brown spots.
Turn and brush the cooked surface lightly with oil.
Turn again and brush the other side with oil.
Cook until golden-brown spots appear and the thepla is cooked through.

learning.did-you-know: Thepla is strongly associated with Gujarati cuisine and is popular as an everyday food as well as a travel food because it keeps well.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to build flavour directly into a flatbread dough using spices, herbs and gram flour.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A versatile Gujarati flatbread that introduces the idea of incorporating herbs, spices and gram flour directly into the dough.
chapman-tips: Kasuri methi can be quite strong, so rub it between your fingers before adding it to release the aroma.
The yogurt helps keep the thepla soft.
The dough should be soft but not sticky.
Roll the thepla thinner than a paratha but slightly thicker than a very thin roti.
Cook over medium-high heat so the bread develops colour without drying out.
Thepla are excellent for using up small amounts of yogurt and herbs.
notes.batch-cooking: Thepla freeze well. Cool completely, stack with baking paper between them if desired and freeze in an airtight bag or container. Reheat directly from frozen in a dry frying pan.
notes.leftovers: Use leftover thepla as wraps or cut into strips and serve with dips.
